phase1.output:
=== Stack (LIFO) ===
Stack: [First, Second, Third]
Peek: Third
Pop: Third
After pop: [First, Second]
Is empty? false
=== Queue (FIFO) ===
Queue: [First, Second, Third]
Peek: First
Poll: First
After poll: [Second, Third]
Is empty? false
=== Deque (Double-ended) ===
Deque: [Zero, First, Second]
Remove First: Zero
Remove Last: Second
After removals: [First]
=== ArrayDeque ===
ArrayDeque as stack: [C, B, A]
Pop: C
ArrayDeque as queue: [10, 20, 30]
Poll: 10
After poll: [20, 30]
=== PriorityQueue ===
PriorityQueue: [5, 10, 20, 30]
Poll in order:
5 10 20 30 
=== Use Cases ===
Stack: Undo operations, back navigation, parsing
Queue: Task scheduling, print jobs, message processing
Deque: Sliding window, palindrome checking
PriorityQueue: Task prioritization, Dijkstra's algorithm
=== Recommendations ===
Stack: Use ArrayDeque over Stack (faster, more efficient)
Queue: Use ArrayDeque over LinkedList for Queue
Deque: Use ArrayDeque over LinkedList when you need both ends
PriorityQueue: Use for sorted access
phase1.explanation:
Stack, Queue, and Deque are data structures with specific access patterns:

Stack (LIFO — Last In, First Out):
• push() — add to top
• pop() — remove from top
• peek() — look at top

Queue (FIFO — First In, First Out):
• offer() — add to end
• poll() — remove from front
• peek() — look at front

Deque (Double-ended queue):
• addFirst() / addLast() — add to either end
• removeFirst() / removeLast() — remove from either end
• offerFirst() / offerLast() — non-throwing add
• pollFirst() / pollLast() — non-throwing remove

PriorityQueue:
• Sorted by natural order or comparator
• poll() returns smallest element
• O(log n) for add/poll

Implementations:
• Stack (legacy) — use ArrayDeque instead
• LinkedList — implements Deque, Queue
• ArrayDeque — best for Stack/Queue (faster than LinkedList)
• PriorityQueue — sorted queue

Recommendations:
• Stack: Deque<Integer> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
• Queue: Queue<Integer> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
• PriorityQueue: PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<>();

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that demonstrates:
1. Create a Stack (using ArrayDeque) and push numbers 1-5, then pop and print all
2. Create a Queue (using ArrayDeque) and offer numbers 10-14, then poll and print all
3. Create a PriorityQueue with numbers: 50, 20, 40, 10, 30 and poll all (should be sorted)
dialogueHints: Stack: Deque<Integer> stack = new ArrayDeque<>(); push() and pop()
Queue: Queue<Integer> queue = new ArrayDeque<>(); offer() and poll()
PriorityQueue: new PriorityQueue<>(); add all; poll in loop until empty
phase3.openingDialogue: Stack, Queue, Deque — choose your access pattern! 🔀
